was a later Yale physician who pioneered cardiac defibrillation

notable.notable_blurb: physician, Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Patient — landmark US medical ethics
narrative_blocks.body: His 1849 book Physician and Patient has been described as the most original United States contribution to medical ethics in the nineteenth century. It argued for honesty with patients, condemned medical deception even when well-intentioned, and shaped American physician-patient communication norms for a generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After twenty-three years practicing medicine in Norwich, Connecticut, he was chosen in 1852 to succeed Dr. Eli Ives as Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. He held the chair until his death, combining teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.

narrative_blocks.header: Vice President of American Medical Association, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected Vice President of the American Medical Association in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine. The AMA at that time was the leading professional voice for orthodox medicine against homeopathy, eclectic medicine, and the prison sanitary movement.

narrative_blocks.header: Worthington Hooker School named for him
narrative_blocks.body: The Worthington Hooker School in New Haven's East Rock neighborhood still bears his name and is part of the city's public school system. The school was named in recognition of his Yale professorship and his presence in New Haven's intellectual establishment.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on 31 January 1855 to Henrietta Whitney Edwards — daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards — also reunited two branches of the Hooker family. Henrietta descended from Mary Hooker (1673-1740) through her great-great-grandmother Sarah Pierpont and her great-great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont, making her and Worthington both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Shaped by Silliman at Yale, Müller in Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: His Yale years (graduated 1825) put him under Benjamin Silliman, the chemistry and natural history professor who championed empirical observation as the basis of American science. Later study of the German anatomist Johannes Müller's comparative physiology shaped Hooker's textbooks on Human Physiology and his rationalist defense of evidence-based therapeutics against homeopathy.

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers framed Eli Ives at Yale, 1817-1852
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother George took the same Yale M.D. degree in 1817 and apprenticed directly with Dr. Eli Ives, then Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. Thirty-five years later in 1852, Worthington was chosen to succeed Ives in that same Yale chair when Ives retired — making the Hooker brothers the bookends of one of New Haven's longest medical legacies.

education.notes: Medical studies in Philadelphia and Cambridge; Harvard M.D. 1829.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Public elementary and middle school in New Haven CT named after him; original 180 Canner Street building erected 1900; new middle school campus opened 2009.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, CT — gravestone reads 'Beloved Physician'

career.notes: Practiced medicine in Norwich, Connecticut for 23 years before his Yale appointment. His wife Mary Ingersoll died here in early 1853 just months after the family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
career.notes: Succeeded Dr. Eli Ives in the chair when Ives resigned. Held the professorship until his death; combined teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.
Elected VP of the AMA in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine.

sources.short: The Ingersolls of Hampshire (Ingersoll family genealogy), entry #98

parents.father_name: John Ingersoll II (1769-1840) of Westfield, Springfield, Savannah GA, and New York City — merchant
parents.mother_name: Elisabeth Martin (1779-1868), survived her daughter Mary by 15 years
parents.mother_research_notes: Per FamilySearch tree (FamilySearch ID KG3H-PHK). NOT the same Elizabeth Martin as X00228 in our tree (X00228 = wife of H00494 Samuel Hooker b.1762, married 1790; Elisabeth Martin Ingersoll b.1779 would have been 11 at that date, ruling out identity).

narrative_blocks.header: Westfield Ingersoll merchant family, married 1830
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 26 April 1808 at Westfield, Massachusetts to John Ingersoll, and married Worthington Hooker on 29 September 1830 in Springfield. Her brother Job Ingersoll later named his youngest son Worthington Hooker Ingersoll (b.31 Oct 1862) in honor of his accomplished brother-in-law.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for her husband Worthington Hooker; she did not live to see it (built 1900, she died 1853).

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1854, Amherst professor — dead at 29
narrative_blocks.body: Worthington Hooker Jr. graduated from Yale College in 1854 and became Professor of Hygienics and Gymnastics at Amherst College. He died January 26, 1863 at age 29 — one year before the end of the Civil War. His father Worthington Hooker M.D. outlived him by four years, dying in 1867.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 37 years after his own 1863 death.
School in New Haven named for her father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 55 years after her own 1845 death.
School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 61 years after his own 1839 infant death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edward died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edward died in 1839 at the age of one. His parents gave the name to a brother born one year later.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 59 years after his own 1841 infant death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edward died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edward died in 1841, in his first year.

cross_connections.display_label: DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT MARRIAGE: Henrietta Whitney Edwards and Prof. Worthington.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for her husband Worthington Hooker; built 1900, six years after her own 1894 death.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, with husband Worthington

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: She and Worthington Hooker (H01407) were both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker when they married 31 January 1855 — Worthington via the direct John Hooker paternal line, Henrietta via Sarah Pierpont (HD0418) and Rev. James Pierpont's marriage to Mary Hooker (H00034) in the 1690s. The couple had no children together, leaving the dual descent uncombined in any biological line.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Gov. Henry Waggaman Edwards (HD2045) served Connecticut as governor 1833-34 and 1835-38, after representing the state in both the US Senate (1823-27) and House of Representatives (1819-23). His mother Frances Ogden was a cousin of Aaron Burr, making Henrietta a cousin once removed of the third Vice President of the United States.

bio.display_name: Unnamed son of Worthington & Henrietta Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Unnamed died young
narrative_blocks.body: Unnamed died in 1856, in his first year.

narrative_blocks.header: Sole surviving child of Worthington Hooker, 1867
narrative_blocks.body: At his father's death in November 1867, ten-year-old Alfred Edwards Hooker was the only surviving child of six — four siblings by his father's first marriage to Mary Ingersoll had all died before 1867, and his unnamed elder brother by Henrietta had died in infancy. Named for his maternal grandfather Connecticut Governor Henry Waggaman Edwards (HD2045), Alfred lived another twenty years and died in 1887 at age thirty.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1782, Springfield lawyer and judge
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1782 and settled at Springfield, MA, where he practiced law and rose to Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and then Judge of Probate at his death. He was a Deacon of the First Congregational Church of Springfield throughout this period.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of American Board for Foreign Missions
narrative_blocks.body: He was an active member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), the first US foreign-missions organization, founded 1810 in Williamstown MA. The Board sent New England missionaries to India, the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii), and Native American tribes — a defining institution of New England Federalist evangelical culture.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father built this house, later moved to Historic Deerfield.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, beside her husband Judge John Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Born to Springfield's mercantile gentry, 1764
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 13 December 1764 to Col. Josiah Dwight — Yale 1736, owner of an iron foundry, a potash manufactory, and 5,000 acres of Hampshire County land — and to Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield. Her father died in 1768 leaving an estate worth £9,458 when she was four; her mother survived to 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Judge John Hooker, December 1790
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 December 1790 in Springfield she married John Hooker, Yale 1782 graduate and son of the Rev. John of Northampton. She bore him ten children between 1791 and 1808, including Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) and Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard (H01403).

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

notable.notable_blurb: Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: He ran potash works and an iron foundry
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale graduate and Hatfield-born merchant, Josiah Dwight built his fortune at Westfield and Springfield through trade, a potash manufactory, and an iron foundry. He was reckoned the richest man in the old County of Hampshire at his death in 1768.

narrative_blocks.header: He judged the county court for eighteen years
narrative_blocks.body: From 1750 until his death Dwight sat as a judge of the Court of Common Pleas for Hampshire County and held the rank of lieutenant-colonel in the militia. His estate was inventoried at more than nine thousand pounds.

narrative_blocks.header: A Yale man of the old Dedham Dwights
narrative_blocks.body: Dwight graduated from Yale in 1736 and came of the Dedham Dwights, a first cousin of the grandfather of Yale's president Timothy Dwight. He kept shop at Westfield before establishing himself at Springfield.

narrative_blocks.header: His two daughters married into prominent families
narrative_blocks.body: By his second wife, Elizabeth Buckminster, Dwight had five children who lived to maturity. His daughter Sarah married Judge John Hooker, and his son Josiah carried on the Springfield line.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this Springfield house about 1754; it now stands at Historic Deerfield.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of the Springfield Dwight line
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield became Josiah Dwight's second wife in 1757 and bore the five children who survived him. She outlived him by thirty years, dying at Springfield in 1798.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST122.primary_name: Yale School of Medicine (Medical Institution of Yale College)
INST122.name: Yale School of Medicine (Medical Institution of Yale College)
INST122.notes:
Founded 1810 as the Medical Institution of Yale College, the fourth oldest medical school in the United States.
